Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time, maybe late evenings after work or whatever else was going on, but it rarely stuck to a schedule. I'd usually end up cramming reading and writing notes in bursts when I felt the pressure building. With six online courses, I was constantly juggling, so organization wasn't really my strong suit this semester.
Q2: It definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be up late for several nights trying to catch up on multiple assignments. Regular study times just didn't happen for me—my life outside school is unpredictable, and I'd fit studying in wherever I could find a gap.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline coming up or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking about something, and that would remind me I needed to check in. I wasn't the type to just browse the LMS out of curiosity—there had to be a reason pulling me there.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more regularly, especially when peer support sessions were happening and people were active in discussions. But those moments were temporary. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do rather than something natural.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and articles and go through them with a highlighter, making my own notes. Discussion posts were surprisingly helpful too—seeing how other students interpreted things gave me different angles I wouldn't have thought of myself.
Q6: I barely watched the videos, honestly. Twenty-five minutes average sounds about right, maybe even generous. I just can't focus on lectures that way—my mind wanders, and I'd rather read the same content in half the time. I also skipped a lot of the optional supplementary stuff because I was already struggling to keep up with requirements.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to an assignment or exam, I'd engage with it. Anything that felt abstract or disconnected from what I was being graded on got pushed aside. Time is limited, resources are tight, and I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Practical application made things feel worthwhile.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're managing six courses with limited time and resources, you triage. Required stuff got done—sometimes barely, sometimes late—but optional things felt like they were designed for students with more breathing room than I had. I'd feel guilty skipping them, but I did anyway.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download readings, print what I could at the library, and work from handwritten notes. I have a whole notebook system going. The LMS was more like a distribution point for materials than where I actually learned. My real study sessions happened at the kitchen table with my notes spread out.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my notes, rewriting key concepts, or working through problems on paper—none of which showed up anywhere online. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person, quizzing each other or talking through confusing topics. That collaborative learning was invisible to the system but really valuable.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd download a PDF, read through it once online to make sure it was the right file, then never open it in the LMS again. All my actual engagement with that material happened on my laptop or printed copies. The LMS would show one quick access, but I might have studied that document for hours over several days.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike, and I'd feel paralyzed—knowing I needed to do things but unable to start. Financial stress didn't help either; worrying about rent makes it hard to focus on coursework. Those weeks, my attendance and engagement dropped significantly.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS felt overwhelming. Too many tabs, too many notifications, too much to process. I'd log in, feel the weight of everything I was behind on, and sometimes just log out without doing anything productive. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it needed to be.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open materials and scroll through them without really absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the text, but my brain was somewhere else—worrying about other things or just too tired to process. Those sessions felt like going through motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot, even though I knew participation mattered. The anxiety of putting my thoughts out there for others to see was real. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I had something smarter to say, and then later never came, or I'd rush something mediocre at the last minute.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the focus I'd been missing all week. It's not healthy, but that pressure was what got me moving. The LMS became much more active for me in the 48 hours before anything was due.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But feedback was sometimes slow, and by the time it came, I'd already moved on mentally. The peer support stuff helped more—getting real-time responses from classmates felt more immediate and encouraging than waiting for instructor comments.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work around my unpredictable schedule, but bad because without rigid structure, I'd drift. I needed more guardrails than the course provided. The freedom to manage my own time meant I often mismanaged it.
Q19: Having everything online was necessary for me given my situation, so that helped. But the sheer volume of materials across six courses made it hard to prioritize. Clearer signposting about what was essential versus supplementary would have helped. Sometimes I'd spend time on something only to realize it wasn't actually important for assessment.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually producing work—which I relate to. You might log in multiple times, open documents, read discussion posts, all while being too overwhelmed or perfectionist to actually submit anything. High activity doesn't mean high productivity when anxiety is involved.
Q21: That's basically me. I did a lot of my learning offline, so my LMS activity looked minimal compared to what I actually studied. My exam score was decent because I knew the material—I just didn't engage with it through the platform. The system can't see what happens in my notebook or in study sessions with friends.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when people log in, what they click on, submission times. That's useful for identifying who's completely absent. But it misses quality of engagement, offline studying, mental state, and whether someone actually understood anything. It's like measuring how often someone opens a book without knowing if they read it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, when you think about course concepts outside of study time, when you want to do well because the content matters to you. For me, engagement fluctuated—sometimes I was genuinely interested, other times I was just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels, how financial stress affected my focus, or the peer support conversations that happened outside the platform. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with physical notes or the genuine learning that happened in face-to-face study groups. My engagement was real, just not always digitally visible.
